game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms III: Dragon of Destiny

design_philosophy.core_loop: Select scenario and warlord(s), manage territories and character stats across multiple Chinese cities, execute turn-based strategic campaigns to conquer all 46 cities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Territorial conquest and resource/character management in a multiplayer sandbox of competing warlords
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command armies as a warlord in ancient China, unifying the Three Kingdoms through strategic warfare and diplomacy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game design prioritizes depth and series-fan appeal over accessibility; skews toward experienced strategy players rather than casual audiences. Multiple scenario starting points allow non-linear approaches to the same historical setting. Heavy emphasis on character statistics and territorial mechanics over narrative progression.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: players choose starting scenario, select which warlord(s) to control, can insert custom characters into unoccupied territories, and determine their own strategic path to total conquest. Game scales with player decision-making rather than preset difficulty modes.

progression.upgrade_system: Character statistics system with six tracked attributes (War Ability, Intelligence, Politics, Charisma, Army Command, Naval Command) that evolve through gameplay
progression.unlock_mechanism: Scenario selection unlocks different initial territorial configurations and warlord rosters; PC-Engine version includes additional Yellow Turban Rebellion scenario (AD 184)
replayability_hooks: Six distinct scenarios with different starting conditions and warlord availability
200+ unique historical characters with different stat distributions
Custom character insertion for personalized playthroughs
Multiple viable warlord choices per scenario

world_building_techniques: Scenario framing based on Romance of the Three Kingdoms novel, establishing historical context (AD 189-235)
Character names and rosters grounded in actual historical figure from source material
Territory and city names reflecting ancient Chinese geography
Warlord allegiances loosely depicting novel events, though gameplay diverges after scenario start

faction_indicators: Warlord allegiances determined at scenario start (Dong Zhuo, Liu Bei, etc.)
Territory control visible on map showing faction expansion
Character recruitment and loyalty mechanics implied by stat-based command system
danger_signals: Military conquest as primary victory condition
Character command statistics reflecting military threat level
environmental_storytelling: 46 distinct cities with unique strategic positions
Historical scenario setup (e.g., 'Dong Zhuo's reign of terror' implies oppressive governance)
Character distribution across territories reflecting faction power

sources_used: wikipedia: Romance of the Three Kingdoms III: Dragon of Destiny
